items.title: Definition of benign febrile seizure
items.contentHtml:
Definition

  • The benign febrile seizure is an epileptic seizure associated with fever >38°C, without infection of the central nervous system or acute electrolyte imbalance, in a previously healthy child.
  • It is classified as simple (generalized, <15 minutes, without recurrence in 24 hours) or complex (focal, prolonged >15 minutes, recurrent in 24 hours).
  • Its pathophysiology involves proinflammatory cytokines (interleukin-1 beta) that lower the seizure threshold in an immature brain.
  • The diagnosis is clinical; It does not require routine studies if it meets the criteria for a simple febrile seizure.

items.title: Typical age of presentation
items.contentHtml:
Typical age

  • The peak incidence of benign febrile seizure occurs between 6 months and 5 years, with maximum frequency around 18 months.
  • Below 6 months, infection of the central nervous system, inborn errors of metabolism or early genetic epilepsy must be ruled out.
  • In people over 5 years of age, the probability of epilepsy underlying or acute symptomatic crisis increases, so expanded evaluation is required.
  • The immaturity of ion channels and GABAergic receptors in this age window favors the appearance of attacks with fever.

items.title: Seizure type: generalized tonic-clonic
items.contentHtml:
Generalized tonic-clonic seizure

  • The typical seizure of simple benign febrile seizure is a generalized tonic-clonic seizure, with loss of consciousness, rigidity and symmetrical rhythmic movements.
  • There should be no signs of focality (sustained ocular deviation, unilateral movements, automatisms) that suggest complex seizures or injury structural.
  • Electrical activity originates simultaneously in both hemispheres; the interictal electroencephalogram is usually normal.
  • The differential diagnosis includes febrile syncope, severe chills or sobbing spasms, which do not present a tonic phase or epileptiform discharge.

items.title: Duration of the crisis
items.contentHtml:
Duration of less than 15 minutes

  • In simple benign febrile seizure, the seizure lasts less than 15 minutes; If it lasts longer, it is considered complex febrile seizure.
  • A duration >15 minutes requires ruling out febrile status epilepticus, which requires urgent pharmacological treatment with benzodiazepines.
  • Most simple febrile seizures last between 1 and 5 minutes and cease spontaneously without intervention.
  • If the seizure lasts longer than 5 minutes, rectal or diazepam should be administered. Intranasal midazolam (dose: 0.2-0.5 mg/kg) to prevent progression.

items.title: No recurrence in 24 hours
items.contentHtml:
No recurrence in 24 hours

  • One of the criteria for simple benign febrile seizure is that there is no more than one seizure in the same febrile episode (no recurrence in 24 hours).
  • If two or more seizures occur in 24 hours, it is classified as complex febrile seizure and the risk of epilepsy increases.
  • Early recurrence may be due to rapid temperature fluctuations or a serious underlying bacterial infection.
  • In case of multiple attacks, lumbar puncture and consideration of neuroimaging studies are recommended.

items.title: Management: no prophylactic anticonvulsants
items.contentHtml:
Does not require prophylactic anticonvulsants

  • The simple benign febrile seizure does not require prophylactic anticonvulsants, since the risk of subsequent epilepsy is low (<2% in simple).
  • Intermittent treatment with oral diazepam (0.3 mg/kg every 8 hours during fever) can reduce recurrences, but not It is routinely indicated due to adverse effects (sedation, ataxia).
  • Continuous anticonvulsants (phenobarbital, valproic acid) do not prevent epilepsy and have cognitive toxicity, so they are avoided.
  • The indication for prophylaxis is only considered in very frequent complex febrile seizures or in patients with pre-existing neurological disorders.

items.foco: Lumbar puncture if meningitis is suspected
items.title: Indication for lumbar puncture
items.contentHtml:
Lumbar puncture if meningitis is suspected

  • The lumbar puncture is indicated in children with febrile seizure if there are meningeal signs, bulging fontanelle, altered state of consciousness or complex seizure without clear cause.
  • In children under 12 months of age, meningoencephalitis can occur without neck stiffness, so routine lumbar puncture is recommended when first febrile seizure in this group.
  • Between 12 and 18 months, it is evaluated on a case-by-case basis; if there is complete vaccination against Haemophilus influenzae and pneumococcus, the risk is lower.
  • The analysis of the cerebrospinal fluid should include glucose, proteins, cell count and culture; a pleocytosis >5 cells/mm³ suggests infection of the central nervous system.

items.title: Non-routine electroencephalogram
items.contentHtml:
Electroencephalogram not routinely indicated

  • The electroencephalogram is not routinely indicated in simple benign febrile seizure, as it is usually normal and does not predict recurrences or epilepsy.
  • It is reserved for cases of complex febrile seizures, abnormal neurological development or suspicion of genetic epilepsy (such as Dravet).
  • Performing it early (first 24-48 hours) can show non-specific postictal slowness, which has no prognostic value.
  • An electroencephalogram with generalized or focal spike-waves requires reassessing the diagnosis and considering epilepsy.

items.title: Antipyretics and prevention
items.contentHtml:
Antipyretics do not prevent recurrence

  • The use of antipyretics (paracetamol 10-15 mg/kg/dose or ibuprofen 5-10 mg/kg/dose) reduces fever but does not prevent the recurrence of new febrile seizures.
  • Fever is a trigger, but the speed of thermal rise and the temperature peak are not correlated. linearly with seizure risk.
  • Prophylactic administration of antipyretics throughout the febrile episode has not been shown to reduce the incidence of seizures in controlled studies.
  • They are indicated only for the comfort of the child and to treat hyperthermia >38.5°C; They do not modify the seizure threshold.

items.foco: Risk of future epilepsy is low
items.title: Risk of future epilepsy
items.contentHtml:
Risk of future epilepsy is low

  • The risk of future epilepsy after a simple benign febrile seizure is low, approximately 1-2%, similar to that of the general population.
  • In complex febrile seizures, the risk rises to 4-6%, and if there is a family history of epilepsy, it can be as high as 10%.
  • Additional risk factors include: focal seizure, duration >15 minutes, recurrence within 24 hours, and prior abnormal neurodevelopment.
  • No follow-up studies or serial electroencephalograms are required in children with simple seizures; Educating parents about the favorable prognosis is key.

items.title: Differences: simple vs complex
items.contentHtml:
Simple vs complex febrile seizure

  • The simple febrile seizure is generalized, lasts <15 minutes and does not recur within 24 hours; complex febrile seizure is focal, lasts >15 minutes or recurs within 24 hours.
  • Complex seizure has a higher risk of future epilepsy (4-6%) and requires more exhaustive evaluation (neuroimaging, electroencephalogram, lumbar puncture depending on context).
  • Febrile status epilepticus (>30 minutes) is considered a severe variant of complex seizure, with risk of sequelae. neurological.
  • The correct classification determines the need for complementary studies and neurological follow-up.

items.title: Treatment: fever control
items.contentHtml:
Treatment: fever control only

  • The treatment of simple benign febrile seizure is limited to fever control with antipyretics and physical measures (light clothing, cool environment).
  • Anticonvulsants are not administered acutely if the seizure has spontaneously ceased within 5 minutes.
  • If the seizure lasts >5 minutes, benzodiazepine should be started: rectal diazepam (0.5 mg/kg, maximum 10 mg) or intranasal midazolam (0.2 mg/kg).
  • Parent education includes explaining that the seizure does not cause brain damage, that it does not require chronic medication, and when to go to the emergency room (seizure >5 minutes, meningeal signs or recurrence).
